title: Blood on Silk — The Highest-Stakes Protein Stain
stainChemistry: Blood is a complex protein stain dominated by hemoglobin — an iron-containing metalloprotein that binds oxygen. On silk, this is a double problem: hemoglobin's protein chains hydrogen-bond directly to silk's fibroin (itself a protein fiber), and the iron (heme) component oxidizes to Fe³⁺, producing a stubborn brown chromophore that anchors into silk's porous crystalline structure. Heat coagulates the hemoglobin just like cooking an egg — making the bond permanent.
whyThisWorks: Cold water keeps the hemoglobin soluble and prevents coagulation. A neutral lubricant loosens the surface deposit without stressing the delicate silk fiber. The protein formula (mild alkali) breaks the peptide-fiber hydrogen bonds, but on silk you must use a diluted, pH-controlled version — because silk IS a protein fiber and aggressive alkali (pH >8) attacks the fibroin backbone. Acetic acid neutralization after the alkaline step is mandatory to return silk to its safe acidic pH range (4.0–7.0). No protease enzymes — they'd digest the silk right along with the blood.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from back of fabric using water gun at safe distance (6+ inches)
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold — below 80°F. NEVER warm or hot. Heat coagulates hemoglobin irreversibly on silk.
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place silk face-down on clean white towel over vacuum nose. Flush from the back with cold water gun held 6+ inches from fabric. Watch the towel — you should see red/brown transfer. Keep flushing until transfer slows significantly. On silk, use LESS pressure than on cotton — silk fibers weaken when wet and aggressive water pressure causes chafing and seam slippage.

spottingProtocol.technique: Apply with soft sponge, light tamping — no brushing
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply a small amount of neutral synthetic detergent to the stain area. On silk, NEVER use a spotting brush — only soft sponge or cotton pad with light tamping action. Brush bristles break silk's smooth fibroin surface and create visible chafe marks. Tamp gently to work the lubricant into the remaining blood deposit.

spottingProtocol.technique: Apply with dropper, light tamp with pad
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply diluted protein formula via dropper directly onto the stain. The mild alkali breaks hemoglobin's hydrogen bonds to silk fibroin. Watch the color — it should shift from red-brown toward lighter. On silk, limit dwell time to 3 minutes max. Extended alkaline contact weakens silk's peptide bonds. Tamp lightly with cotton pad. Do NOT use full-strength protein formula on silk.
Flush completely to remove all alkaline residue. Residual alkali continues attacking silk fiber after you walk away. This flush is non-negotiable.

Apply dilute acetic acid to neutralize any remaining alkaline residue and return silk to its safe pH range (4.0–7.0). Silk is most stable in mildly acidic conditions. This step also helps remove the iron component of the blood. Blot gently.

spottingProtocol.technique: Final flush, then lay flat to dry on clean towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final cold water flush. Lay silk flat on a clean dry towel — NEVER hang wet silk (stretches and distorts). Do not use heat. If a faint brown shadow remains after drying, one careful application of 3% hydrogen peroxide (test first in hidden area — silk is sensitive to peroxide) can address residual iron staining. Apply for no more than 60 seconds, then flush immediately.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Immediately flush with COLD water from the back of the fabric. Blot — never rub. Cold water alone removes the majority of fresh blood on silk if you act within minutes. Do NOT use soap yet.

SET: Soak in cold water with a few drops of mild pH-neutral liquid dish soap (Dawn Free & Clear — no dyes, no fragrance) for 15–20 minutes. Gently agitate with fingers. Rinse cold. Repeat if needed. Do NOT use enzyme-based detergent (Tide, OxiClean) on silk — protease enzymes digest silk fiber.

STOP AND CALL A PRO: If the blood has dried or been heat-set, or if any brown shadow remains after two cold-water treatments, take it to a professional dry cleaner. Tell them exactly what happened and that you kept it cold. Do not attempt hydrogen peroxide at home on silk — the margin between effective and damaging is too narrow without professional training.
materialWarnings: NEVER hot water on blood + silk — heat coagulates hemoglobin permanently into the fiber. This is the #1 most common mistake.
NEVER protease enzymes on silk — silk IS a protein fiber (fibroin). Protease digests the silk itself, causing irreversible holes and fiber dissolution.
NEVER chlorine bleach on silk — destroys protein structure and causes severe yellowing that cannot be reversed.
NEVER alcohol (rubbing alcohol, hand sanitizer, hairspray) on blood — alcohol sets protein stains permanently, per Dan Eisen's research.
NEVER brush silk — use only soft pad or sponge. Brush bristles break silk's smooth surface and create visible chafe marks.
NEVER use full-strength alkaline spotter on silk — pH above 8.0 degrades silk fibroin. Always dilute protein formula 1:1 minimum.

professional.name: Street's Protein Formula
professional.use: Primary protein stain treatment — dilute 1:1 for silk
professional.note: Mild alkaline protein spotter. On silk, limit dwell to 3 minutes max and flush immediately. Test dye safety first — silk dyes are often acid dyes that shift in alkaline conditions.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG
professional.use: Not applicable for blood (blood is wet-side, not dry-side)
professional.note: Only use if blood stain has an oily co-component (e.g., blood + food grease). Apply POG first for the oil portion only, then switch to wet-side protein protocol.

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
professional.use: Last resort for residual brown iron shadow after protein removal
professional.note: Apply for 60 seconds maximum on silk. Test first. Silk tolerates brief 3% peroxide contact but prolonged exposure weakens the fiber. Flush immediately.

consumer.name: Dawn Free & Clear Dish Soap
consumer.use: Mild surfactant for cold-water soak on fresh blood
consumer.note: A few drops in cold water. No dyes, no fragrance, no enzymes. Safe for silk. Soak 15–20 minutes, agitate gently with fingers.

consumer.name: White distilled vinegar
consumer.use: Acid rinse after any alkaline treatment to protect silk
consumer.note: 1 tablespoon per cup of cold water as a final rinse. Returns silk to its safe acidic pH range.

escalation.when: Blood has dried and set, any brown shadow persists after two cold-water treatments, or the silk has been accidentally heated (ironed, dried, washed in warm water) while blood was present.
escalation.whatToTell: Tell the cleaner: 'This is blood on silk. I kept it cold and did not use heat, enzymes, or bleach. The stain has been here for [X days]. I flushed with cold water but a brown shadow remains — likely iron oxidation from the hemoglobin.'
escalation.specialistType: Dry cleaner with wet cleaning capability — silk needs to stay in the hands of someone who understands protein fiber pH sensitivity. If silk has visible fiber damage, refer to a textile restoration specialist.

solventNote: For dry cleaners: Pre-spot blood on silk BEFORE loading into the machine. Blood will set permanently at drycleaning temperatures (PERC at 140°F, HC at 130°F). Use cold protein formula pre-spot, flush, neutralize with acetic acid, then clean. Hydrocarbon solvent is gentler on silk than PERC — less odor retention and better hand-feel preservation. GreenEarth (D5 silicone) is the ideal solvent for luxury silk if available.
